summary: Evans advocates for debugging by dropping into a REPL at a breakpoint, showing how this works in Python (via ipdb), Ruby (via pry), and even partially in C (via gdb). She demonstrates the technique with concrete code examples, showing how you get access to all in-scope variables and functions at the breakpoint. She contrasts this with traditional debuggers, noting that REPL debugging is easier to remember because you just set a breakpoint and start running code interactively. The post positions this as a middle ground between print-statement debugging and full debugger usage.
key_points: Setting a breakpoint that drops you into a REPL gives you both in-scope variables and access to all your program's functions interactively
Python's ipdb.set_trace() and Ruby's binding.pry are the practical ways to get this experience in those languages
GDB provides a surprisingly REPL-like experience for C thanks to extraordinary engineering effort, even though C is compiled
REPL debugging is easier to remember than traditional debugger workflows, especially for people who switch languages frequently
This technique works in interpreted languages but generally not in compiled ones, with GDB as a notable partial exception
money_quotes: I like doing this because it gives me both: all the variables in scope at the breakpoint, so I can print them out interactively, and easy access to all the functions in my program, so I can call them to try to find issues.
This stuff only works in gdb because the gdb developers put in a lot of work doing Very Weird Things to make it easier to get a REPL-like experience.
This is the only way I use gdb when looking at C programs – I never set watchpoints or do anything fancy, I just set a couple of breakpoints in the program and then poke around at those points.
I think part of the reason I like this type of REPL debugging more than using a more traditional debugger is – it's so easy to remember how to do it!

With debuggers, I always forget how to use the debugger (probably partly because I switch programming languages a lot) and I get confused about what features it has and how they work, so I never use it.
key_insight: Dropping into a REPL at a breakpoint combines the simplicity of print-statement debugging with the power of a full debugger, and its low cognitive overhead makes it a technique you'll actually use.
